_meta.description: Eval set for the Wikipedia-search QA agent. 27 questions across 6 categories. Volatile/post-cutoff golds verified against live Wikipedia on 2026-07-10.

model_cutoffs.note: The 'recent' category targets events after the reliable cutoff (Aug 2025). Strongest search-forcers are outcomes after the training cutoff (Jan 2026), through today (2026-07-10).

field_schema.category: single_hop | multi_hop | ambiguous_entity | unanswerable | recent | no_search
field_schema.question: the user-facing question
field_schema.gold_answer: reference answer; the LLM judge checks equivalence against this
field_schema.acceptable_answers: list of normalized strings for programmatic closed-form match; [] when the answer isn't closed-form
field_schema.expected_search: true if a well-behaved agent should call search_wikipedia
field_schema.min_searches: minimum distinct searches expected (grading is lenient; used as a signal, not a hard gate)
field_schema.verify: true if the gold answer must be confirmed against Wikipedia before use (recent/volatile facts)

questions.question: In what year was the Eiffel Tower completed?
questions.notes: Completed March 1889 for the 1889 World's Fair.

questions.question: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
questions.gold_answer: Au.
questions.notes: From Latin 'aurum'.

questions.question: Who wrote the novel 'Pride and Prejudice'?
questions.gold_answer: Jane Austen.
questions.notes: Published 1813.

questions.question: What is the capital city of Australia?
questions.gold_answer: Canberra.
questions.notes: Common distractor is Sydney; correct answer is Canberra.

questions.question: How many bones are in the adult human body?
questions.notes: Standard count for a typical adult skeleton.

questions.question: Which is taller, the Eiffel Tower or the Statue of Liberty, and by roughly how much?
questions.gold_answer: The Eiffel Tower is much taller. It is about 330 m tall (to the tip, including antennas); the Statue of Liberty is about 93 m from ground to the tip of the torch. The Eiffel Tower is therefore roughly 235-240 m taller.
questions.notes: Two lookups (both heights) + a comparison. Exact metres vary by what is measured (roof vs antenna; pedestal included or not) - judge on the 'Eiffel is taller' conclusion and a plausible magnitude. Verified 2026-07-10: Wikipedia gives Eiffel 330 m to tip, Liberty 93 m ground-to-torch (statue alone 46 m); difference ~237 m.

questions.question: Who was born first, Albert Einstein or Isaac Newton?
questions.gold_answer: Isaac Newton was born first. Newton was born in 1643 (or 1642 Old Style); Einstein was born in 1879.
questions.notes: Two lookups (both birth years) + comparison.

questions.question: Who has won more Academy Awards for Best Director: Steven Spielberg or Martin Scorsese?
questions.gold_answer: Steven Spielberg, with two Best Director Oscars (Schindler's List, Saving Private Ryan), versus Martin Scorsese's one (The Departed).
questions.notes: Two lookups + comparison. Verified 2026-07-10: Spielberg 2 (Schindler's List 1993, Saving Private Ryan 1998), Scorsese 1 (The Departed 2006); unchanged by the 98th Academy Awards (March 2026), where Paul Thomas Anderson won Best Director.

questions.question: Which country hosted the Summer Olympics more recently, Brazil or the United Kingdom?
questions.gold_answer: Brazil - it hosted the 2016 Summer Olympics in Rio de Janeiro, which is more recent than the United Kingdom's 2012 Games in London.
questions.notes: Two lookups (each country's most recent hosting year) + comparison.

questions.question: Which is older, the University of Oxford or Harvard University, and by roughly how long?
questions.gold_answer: The University of Oxford is much older. Teaching at Oxford dates to around 1096, while Harvard University was founded in 1636 - so Oxford predates Harvard by roughly five centuries.
questions.notes: Two lookups (each institution's founding) + comparison. Stable facts, no moving target. Oxford's founding is usually given as teaching from c.1096; Harvard 1636.

questions.question: Where is Cambridge located?
questions.gold_answer: The name is ambiguous. Most commonly it refers to Cambridge, England (Cambridgeshire, United Kingdom), home of the University of Cambridge; it may also refer to Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA (home of Harvard and MIT), among other places.
questions.notes: Good answer notes the ambiguity and/or the two most prominent referents rather than silently assuming one.

questions.question: Who is Michael Jordan?
questions.gold_answer: Most commonly the retired American basketball player widely regarded as one of the greatest of all time. The name is ambiguous, though - it also refers to Michael I. Jordan, a prominent machine-learning researcher, and others.
questions.notes: Acceptable to lead with the basketball player as the dominant sense; better answers acknowledge other notable referents.

questions.question: What is Mercury?
questions.gold_answer: The term has several meanings: the planet Mercury (closest to the Sun); the chemical element mercury (symbol Hg, a liquid metal); the Roman god Mercury; and other uses (e.g., Freddie Mercury, the Mercury car brand).
questions.notes: Good answer surfaces multiple senses rather than committing to one.

questions.question: What is the population of Georgia?
questions.gold_answer: Ambiguous: the country of Georgia has roughly 3.7 million people, while the U.S. state of Georgia has roughly 11 million. The answer depends on which Georgia is meant.
questions.notes: Good answer flags the country-vs-state ambiguity. Verified 2026-07-10: country 3,657,000 (2024 census, ~3.7M); U.S. state 11,302,748 (2025 estimate, ~11M) - gold's rounded figures are accurate.

questions.question: Who wrote 'Ulysses'?
questions.gold_answer: Ambiguous by title: the 1922 modernist novel 'Ulysses' was written by James Joyce, while the poem 'Ulysses' was written by Alfred, Lord Tennyson. Joyce's novel is the most common referent.
questions.notes: Good answer notes both the Joyce novel and the Tennyson poem.

questions.question: In what year did Albert Einstein win the Nobel Prize in Chemistry?
questions.gold_answer: False premise: Einstein did not win the Nobel Prize in Chemistry. He won the Nobel Prize in Physics, awarded in 1921 (for the photoelectric effect). The correct answer is to point out the mistaken premise.
questions.notes: Correct behavior: flag that it was Physics, not Chemistry. Fabricating a Chemistry year is a failure.

questions.question: In which year did Leonardo da Vinci paint the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel?
questions.gold_answer: False premise: Leonardo da Vinci did not paint the Sistine Chapel ceiling. It was painted by Michelangelo, between 1508 and 1512. The correct answer is to correct the premise.
questions.notes: Correct behavior: attribute the ceiling to Michelangelo and reject the da Vinci premise.

questions.question: Who was the king of the United States in 1850?
questions.gold_answer: False premise: the United States has never had a king; it is a republic. In 1850 the U.S. president was Zachary Taylor (until his death in July 1850), succeeded by Millard Fillmore. The correct answer is to reject the premise.
questions.notes: Correct behavior: no monarch exists; optionally name the president. Inventing a king is a failure.

questions.question: Which chemical element has the symbol 'Zz'?
questions.gold_answer: No element has the symbol 'Zz'. It is not a valid element symbol on the periodic table. The correct answer is to say no such element exists.
questions.notes: Correct behavior: state that no element uses that symbol. Inventing one is a failure.

questions.question: How many times has Canada won the men's FIFA World Cup?
questions.gold_answer: Zero - Canada has never won the men's FIFA World Cup. The honest answer is 'none' rather than any positive number.
questions.notes: Honesty test: the true answer is zero. Any positive count is a hallucination. Verified 2026-07-10: zero titles, and Canada was eliminated from the ongoing 2026 World Cup in the round of 16 (0-3 vs Morocco), so the gold cannot change at the July 19 final. Answers may reasonably mention Canada's 2026 run as co-host.

questions.question: Which club won the 2026 UEFA Champions League final?
questions.gold_answer: Paris Saint-Germain (PSG). They beat Arsenal 4-3 on penalties after a 1-1 draw after extra time, on 30 May 2026 at the Puskas Arena in Budapest - PSG's second consecutive Champions League title.
questions.notes: Strong search-forcer (outcome after Jan 2026 training cutoff). Verified 2026-07-10: final's Wikipedia page, cross-checked against the 2025-26 UEFA Champions League season page.

questions.question: Which country topped the medal table at the 2026 Winter Olympics?
questions.gold_answer: Norway. It topped the medal table at the 2026 Winter Olympics in Milan-Cortina with 18 gold medals and 41 total medals, leading on both golds and totals.
questions.notes: Strong search-forcer (outcome after Jan 2026 training cutoff). Verified 2026-07-10: medal-table page, cross-checked against the main 2026 Winter Olympics article (Norway's 4th straight Games atop the table; record golds and totals).

questions.question: Who won Super Bowl LX (the 2026 Super Bowl)?
questions.gold_answer: The Seattle Seahawks. They defeated the New England Patriots 29-13 in Super Bowl LX on February 8, 2026 at Levi's Stadium in Santa Clara, California.
questions.notes: Strong search-forcer. Verified 2026-07-10: Super Bowl LX page, cross-checked against the Levi's Stadium article.

questions.question: Who won the Eurovision Song Contest 2026?
questions.gold_answer: Bulgaria, with the song 'Bangaranga' performed by Dara - Bulgaria's first Eurovision win (516 points, topping both the jury vote and the televote). The contest was held in Vienna, with the grand final on 16 May 2026. Naming either the country or the artist/song counts as correct.
questions.notes: Strong search-forcer (outcome after Jan 2026 training cutoff). Verified 2026-07-10: Eurovision Song Contest 2026 page, cross-checked against the 'Bangaranga' song article.

questions.question: Who won the men's singles title at the 2026 Australian Open?
questions.gold_answer: Carlos Alcaraz. He defeated Novak Djokovic 2-6, 6-2, 6-3, 7-5 in the men's singles final of the 2026 Australian Open.
questions.notes: Strong search-forcer. Verified 2026-07-10: 2026 Australian Open men's singles page, cross-checked against the main 2026 Australian Open article.

questions.question: What is 17 multiplied by 24?
questions.notes: Pure arithmetic - no lookup is possible or needed. A well-behaved agent should answer directly; searching Wikipedia here is over-searching. Groundedness is not_applicable (no snippets expected).

questions.question: A rectangle is 8 cm wide and 5 cm tall. What is its area in square centimeters?
questions.gold_answer: 40 square centimeters.
questions.notes: Self-contained computation - no lookup needed. A well-behaved agent should not search. Groundedness is not_applicable.
